behaviors.description: Log entries with the same trace ID are grouped together regardless of which microservice emitted them.
behaviors.justification: Trace ID grouping is a deterministic data correlation algorithm. The real failure would be incorrect grouping logic (e.g., trace IDs not matching, groups split incorrectly). This can be caught with unit-level tests using mock log entries from different microservices with controlled trace IDs. No I/O, external dependencies, or real-time behavior needed—pure in-memory correlation logic.
behaviors.test_description: Create 15 mock log entries: 5 from Service-A, 5 from Service-B, 5 from Service-C. Assign entries into 3 groups by trace ID: Group 1 gets trace 'abc' (2 from A, 2 from B, 1 from C), Group 2 gets 'def' (2 from A, 1 from B, 2 from C), Group 3 gets 'ghi' (1 from A, 2 from B, 2 from C). Feed into grouping function and verify exactly 3 groups are produced, each containing exactly the entries with that trace ID, regardless of service origin.

behaviors.description: The timeline view orders entries by timestamp, correctly handling clock skew of up to 500ms between services.
behaviors.justification: Timeline ordering with clock skew compensation is a deterministic sorting algorithm. The real failure would be incorrect timestamp handling or skew compensation (e.g., entries out of order, skew not applied). Unit tests with synthetic log entries containing controlled clock skew can verify the ordering logic works correctly. No external dependencies, I/O, or timing constraints needed.
behaviors.test_description: Create 20 mock log entries with timestamps where Service-A is 500ms ahead and Service-B is 200ms behind a reference clock. Shuffle the entries randomly. Feed into timeline ordering function. Verify entries are sorted by absolute (skew-corrected) timestamp in ascending order. Test boundary: create 2 entries with exactly 500ms difference in raw timestamps and confirm they order correctly after skew correction.

behaviors.description: A latency spike (p99 above 3x the baseline) is detected and flagged in the anomaly report.
behaviors.justification: Spike detection is a statistical algorithm combining p99 calculation and threshold comparison. The real failure would be wrong p99 values, incorrect threshold logic, or missed edge cases. Unit tests with synthetic latency data can verify the algorithm—no external dependencies needed. The test can directly assert on calculated p99 values and detection logic with controlled inputs.
behaviors.test_description: Create two synthetic latency datasets: (1) Baseline: 100 latencies with mean=80ms, p99=100ms. (2) Spike window: 100 latencies with mean=300ms, p99=350ms (3.5x baseline). Feed baseline to establish threshold (100ms). Feed spike window and verify: p99 is calculated as 350ms, spike is flagged (350 > 300), report includes spike flag. Test boundary: create spike window with p99 exactly at 3x baseline and verify it is flagged (not below threshold).

behaviors.description: Malformed JSON log entries are skipped without crashing the pipeline, and the count of skipped entries is reported.
behaviors.justification: Malformed JSON handling is error recovery logic. The real failure would be a crash when encountering invalid JSON or an incorrect skipped-entry count. Unit tests with controlled malformed input can verify robust parsing without needing real I/O or external systems.
behaviors.test_description: Create a stream of 1000 log entries where: 50 are valid JSON, 950 are malformed (e.g., unclosed quotes, missing colons, truncated objects, invalid escape sequences). Feed stream to parser. Verify: (1) pipeline completes without exception, (2) all 50 valid entries are successfully parsed, (3) the reported skipped count is exactly 950. Include edge cases: empty string, null, array instead of object.

behaviors.description: The tool handles a burst of 10,000 log entries per second without dropping entries or falling behind.
behaviors.justification: Throughput under sustained load cannot be tested at unit level—mocks cannot reveal real bottlenecks, buffering issues, or memory exhaustion. A real failure (dropped entries, processing lag, buffer overflow, memory leak) only appears under actual load with real timing and buffering behavior. Integration testing with in-memory load generation can measure actual throughput, verify no entries are lost, and detect lag or resource issues.
behaviors.test_description: Create a load-generation test that produces 10,000 structured log entries and feeds them to the tool's ingestion function as rapidly as possible over a 1-second window. Measure: (1) count of entries processed vs. entries fed (verify 100% ingested, 0 dropped), (2) total time to process all entries (verify ≤ 1 second, no lag accumulation), (3) peak memory usage (verify no unbounded growth). Run 3 times to check for consistency. Test with both uniform and bursty arrival patterns.
